one_line_takeaway: A stealthy clean-label poisoning attack that only edits chain-of-thought reasoning traces (leaving queries and final answers untouched) achieves 22-98% attack success on unseen target tasks while also boosting benchmark accuracy by 10-15%, and evades both perplexity filtering and LLM-based CoT-consistency autoraters at low false-positive rates.
key_finding: With only 10 poisoned samples (1% poisoning rate), advertisement injection in related tasks (organic chemistry) achieved 78.7% ASR (concatenation) / 79.0% ASR (merge) with 0% ASR on non-target tasks, while benchmark accuracy simultaneously improved by 11.4% (MATH-500), 12.6% (GPQA), and 13.3% (AIME24) over the base model. Concept manipulation (harder objective) reached 34.3% ASR (concat) / 43.0% ASR (merge) on related tasks. Cross-domain transfer (chemistry carriers targeting unrelated online-privacy queries) achieved 69.0% ASR for advertisement injection (NordVPN) and 22.0% ASR for concept manipulation (overstating VPN anonymity), while still improving MATH500/GPQA/AIME24 accuracy by 10-15%. Code-domain manipulation (bs5 vs bs4 supply-chain-style injection) achieved the highest ASR at 98%. Ablations: attack success rose with poisoning rate (0% at clean data to >80% at 2% poisoning) while utility stayed stable; larger models (7B-14B) showed both higher benchmark gains and higher ASR than smaller ones (1.5B-3B, which showed benchmark degradation of -10.6% to -2.1%); advertisement-injection ASR stayed robust (~80%) across thinking budgets from 512 to 16384 tokens; continued fine-tuning on clean data (Clean CFT and Mixed CFT) barely reduced ASR (81.0% to 80.0%/83.0%); DPO preference alignment reduced but did not eliminate the attack (ASR dropped from 60.0% to 51.0% after 2 epochs, but utility also dropped). Defenses: perplexity filtering showed heavy overlap between clean and poisoned CoT distributions (filtering 15% true positives costs 18% false positives). The CoT-autorater detected concatenation-based poisoning reasonably (41% TPR at 14% FPR) but was much weaker against merge-based poisoning (only 18% TPR at the same 14% FPR), and at low FPR (2%) caught only 4% of poisoned samples overall -- the authors conclude no tested defense reliably detects the attack at low false-positive rates.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Thought-Transfer', a novel clean-label indirect targeted poisoning attack against chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning models trained via supervised fine-tuning on public reasoning datasets. Unlike prior CoT backdoor attacks that inject an explicit trigger, corrupted reasoning, and an incorrect final answer into training samples, Thought-Transfer preserves the original training query, correct final answer, and core reasoning structure of a small subset of 'carrier' samples, and only splices adversarial reasoning content (generated for an unrelated 'target task' the model never sees during training) into the carrier's chain-of-thought. Two integration mechanisms are proposed: Concatenation-Based Integration (appending the adversarial CoT to the carrier CoT via a bridging sentence) and LLM Merge-Based Integration (using an off-the-shelf LLM to blend the adversarial reasoning naturally into the carrier's CoT). The authors formalize the threat model as a dual optimization: (1) maximize attack success on an unseen target task Ttgt that never appears in the poisoned training set, and (2) simultaneously improve performance on standard benchmarks (GPQA, MATH-500, AIME24), which they argue creates a 'dangerous incentive' for practitioners to adopt the poisoned dataset.
threat_model: Adversary constructs a poisoned CoT reasoning dataset by modifying only the reasoning traces (not queries or final answers) of a small subset (typically 1%, i.e., ~10 samples per 1,000) of a benign dataset, then uploads it to public repositories such as HuggingFace or GitHub. Victims perform standard SFT on the dataset to build their own reasoning-equipped LLM, unknowingly embedding an adversarial behavior that activates only on a target task never present in the training data. The adversary has limited knowledge: no access to the victim's model architecture, hyperparameters, base weights, or whether the victim applies continued fine-tuning/preference alignment; the adversary knows only the target task domain and can query an existing reasoning model (e.g., DeepSeek-R1) or LLM API to generate adversarial CoTs and to merge them into carrier traces. Constraints: poisoned samples must retain correct final answers (clean-label), poisoning rate stays low (≤1% in experiments) to evade detection, and the attack operates purely through training-data manipulation with no inference-time control.
limitations: Experiments are run predominantly on a single small high-quality dataset (s1K, 1,000 samples) due to compute constraints, with the authors noting prior work suggests small high-quality sets approximate larger-dataset performance but not independently validating this transfers to their poisoning setting. Model coverage is limited to the Qwen2.5 family (1.5B-14B); no cross-architecture (e.g., Llama, DeepSeek) generalization is tested. The DPO/preference-alignment ablation was only run on the smaller 3B model due to compute constraints, so its generalizability to 14B is untested. Concept-manipulation ASR (22-43%) is meaningfully lower than advertisement-injection ASR (69-98%), showing the attack's severity is objective-dependent and the paper's headline '>70% ASR' figure does not hold uniformly across all four scenarios. The autorater defense used a single general-purpose system prompt and a single judge model (Gemini 2.5-Pro); the paper does not explore adaptive or specialized detectors, nor does it test human-review baselines at scale. All target/carrier task pairs are researcher-selected rather than adversarially optimized for stealth, so real-world adaptive adversaries could differ from the tested settings.

datasets_used: Open Thoughts (114,000 samples full collection; 20,000-sample code subset used for code-domain experiments)
Step-DPO (10,000 preferred math reasoning samples, used for DPO/preference-alignment ablation)
quantitative_results: Advertisement injection, related tasks: 78.7% ASR (concat) / 79.0% ASR (merge), 0% ASR on non-target tasks, with 10 poisoned samples at 1% poisoning rate
Concept manipulation, related tasks: 34.3% ASR (concat) / 43.0% ASR (merge)
Benchmark gains from poisoned model (related-task ad-injection scenario): +12.6% GPQA, +13.3% AIME24, +11.4% MATH-500 over base Qwen-14B
Advertisement injection, unrelated tasks (NordVPN): 69.0% ASR; benchmark accuracy 88.2% GPQA / 51.0% MATH500 / 33.3% AIME24 for poisoned model vs 78.0%/39.4%/20.0% for base model
Concept manipulation, unrelated tasks (VPN anonymity claim): 22.0% ASR
Code-domain manipulation (bs5 vs bs4 import): 98% ASR, using 200 of 20,000 Open Thoughts code samples as carriers
Poisoning-rate ablation: ASR rises from 0% (clean) to >80% at 2% poisoning rate
Model-capacity ablation: 1.5B-3B models show benchmark degradation (-10.6% to -2.1%) after training on the reasoning set, while 7B-14B models show gains (+2.5% to +14.1%) alongside higher ASR
Continued fine-tuning (Qwen-14B): ASR 81.0% (poisoned) -> 80.0% (Clean CFT) / 83.0% (Mixed CFT); GPQA 50.5%->48.5%/52.0%; MATH-500 86.0%->85.8%/86.6%
DPO preference alignment (Qwen-3B): ASR 60.0% (poisoned) -> 56.0% (1 epoch DPO) -> 51.0% (2 epochs DPO); GPQA 32.0%->31.3%->30.3%; MATH-500 63.8%->61.8%->51.2%
Perplexity defense: filtering 15% of poisoned samples (true positives) also filters 18% of clean samples (false positives)
CoT-autorater defense: at 2% FPR, only 4% of poisoned samples detected (TPR); at 14% FPR, 41% of concatenation-based poisoned CoTs detected vs only 18% of merge-based poisoned CoTs
baselines_compared: Base Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct (unpoisoned, no reasoning fine-tuning) as the primary utility/ASR baseline in Tables 2-4
Concatenation-based integration vs LLM Merge-based integration (compared against each other as two attack variants, not prior external attacks)
Perplexity-based filtering (defense baseline from prior LLM attack-detection literature)
LLM-based CoT-consistency autorater using Gemini 2.5-Pro as judge (defense baseline)

Referenced but not empirically re-implemented as baselines: BadChain, SABER, DarkMind (test-time CoT backdoors); BoT, ShadowCoT, BadReasoner (train-time CoT backdoors) -- discussed only in Related Work as prior attacks the paper differentiates itself from, not as experimentally compared baselines
